cards.front: Steps to draw the AC equivalent circuit?
cards.back:
1. Short-circuit all capacitors (C_in, C_out, C_E)
2. Replace DC voltage sources with short circuits
3. Replace DC current sources with open circuits
4. Redraw the simplified circuit

cards.front: How does the R_E reflection trick work?
cards.back: R_E in the emitter (carrying ≈ βi_b) can be moved to the base branch as β·R_E (carrying i_b). This preserves KVL: same voltage drop, but now in the base loop for easier analysis.

cards.front: For h_ie = 1kΩ, β = 50, R_E1 = 240Ω, what is R_base?
Quick formula for CE voltage gain?
cards.back: A_V ≈ -(R_C ∥ R_L) / R_E,unbypassed. Only the unbypassed emitter resistance matters because the bypassed R_E2 is shorted by C_E at signal frequencies.

cards.front: Why is R_out = R_C for a CE amplifier?
cards.back: Looking into the collector with V_S = 0 and R_L removed, you see R_C in parallel with the current source (infinite impedance). If 1/h_oe is also neglected, only R_C remains.

cards.front: Why is A_I less than β?
cards.back: Two losses: (1) I_in splits between I_B and bias resistor current I_1 → not all input current reaches the base. (2) I_C splits between R_C and R_L → not all collector current reaches the load.

cards.front: How does the AC load line differ from the DC load line?
cards.back: AC load line uses R_AC = R_E1 + R_C∥R_L (capacitors shorted, R_L connected). DC load line uses R_DC = R_E1 + R_E2 + R_C (capacitors open, no R_L). AC line is steeper.
When 1/h_oe >> R_C∥R_L. Typical: 1/h_oe = 40 kΩ vs R_C∥R_L = 5 kΩ. The ratio is 8:1, so < 13% error — acceptable for engineering.

cards.front: How to use CE parameters for a CB circuit?
cards.back: Don't convert! Insert the CE equivalent circuit, connect B→B, C→C, E→E. Then analyse with KVL/KCL. The circuit topology handles the configuration difference.

1. DC analysis → Q-point
2. AC equivalent circuit
3. AC load line
4. h-parameter model (simplify)
5. R_E reflection
6. Calculate R_in, R_out, A_V, A_I
7. Sanity check with shortcuts
